Heather, are there any books for kids out there that gets into evolution and, you know, the controversies that are going on now?
There - I mean there's always a couple of books each year on evolution.

It can be kind of a difficult subject to do in a children's science book because generally, you know, children's science books take a real general look at things, and it's hard to talk about evolution without kind of promoting misconceptions.
I mean if a book talks about evolution and it uses words like need or try or want, you know, it's really promoting a misconception that evolution is goal-directed or driven by a need.
So it's kind of hard to kind of bring that subject down to the level of young children, so...
That's interesting.

Phil Manning, let's say you want to brush up on the science behind some of today's hot topics, the hot-button issues that we've been talking about - global warming, stem cell research, or evolution - are there are any books that do a good job of laying out the science behind these issues?
Indeed, there are.
In fact, there's so many good ones it's really hard to figure out where to start.
But two that I'm very familiar with and highly recommend - one of them is called "Mendel in the Kitchen," and it's a scientist's view of genetically modified foods.
It was written by two women, one, Nancy Marie Brown, who's a science writer, and Nina Fedoroff, who's a professor at Penn State University.

And it explains - it makes the case - you know, Prince Charles, I think it was; or somebody like Prince Charles, anyway -said that, you know, they labeled these foods - they labeled them frankenfoods, and that label really stuck...
(Soundbite of laughter)
...and it really set back the work on genetically modified foods quite a bit.
But there are some real opportunities out there to help - for scientists to help feed mankind and to feed them in a better way.

This idea of golden rice, which is a genetically modified rice that provides a precursor for vitamins for children in countries where they eat a lot of rice and many of them go blind because of vitamin deficiencies.
Mm-hmm.
This can fix that.

And so she argues in the book - very forcefully and very cleverly, I think - that the restrictions, the many, many restrictions on genetically modified food should be at least, if not removed, at least modified and to make them a little bit less stringent.
Sometimes the glue is not dry yet, and the boat starts to break in the middle of the sea.
The other book along the same lines is called "The Proteus Effect.
This one I think is even - is a much more - even hotter topic than that, which is about stem cells and their promise for medicine.

And they explain very clearly in here what stem cells are, why they should work, why we should be funding stem cell research and take a few nice shots at our current administration for not doing that.
